summary: Pen name of Hector Hugh Munro, an Edwardian British short story writer known for dark humor and twist endings. His pen name comes from the Rubaiyat's word for 'cupbearer.' Major stories include 'The Open Window,' 'Sredni Vashtar,' 'The Interlopers,' 'Tobermory,' and 'The Lumber Room.' Recurring characters include Clovis Sangrail and Reginald.

works.name: The Open Window
works.description: Saki's most famous story. Vera lies to the nervous Framton Nuttel about the deaths of three hunters and a dog, claiming they disappeared into a bog. When the hunters return through the open window, Nuttel flees in terror. The story ends with the iconic line 'Romance at short notice was her specialty.' Vera smoothly explains Nuttel's departure as due to a 'horror of dogs.'

clues.clue: 'Romance at short notice was her specialty' — ending line
examples: ends with the line 'Romance at short notice was her specialty'

clues.clue: Vera/girl tricks Framton Nuttel into thinking returning hunters are ghosts
examples: Vera pretends that a group of returning hunters are ghosts to scare Framton Nuttel

clues.clue: Three hunters and a dog return through the title window

works.name: Sredni Vashtar
works.description: A boy named Conradin forms a cult around a polecat-ferret named Sredni Vashtar, toasting bread as a form of worship. He steals powdered nutmeg as a reward for causing a toothache. Mrs. De Ropp, his guardian, is killed by the ferret, and the story ends with Conradin calmly making toast while hearing the screams of his maid.

clues.clue: Boy Conradin worships a polecat-ferret that kills his guardian Mrs. De Ropp
examples: Conradin, who worships a ferret that kills his aunt

clues.clue: Boy calmly makes toast as the story ends / hears maid's screams
examples: a boy making a piece of toast after Mrs. De Ropp is killed by the title polecat-ferret

clues.clue: Boy steals powdered nutmeg as reward for causing a toothache
examples: a boy steals powdered nutmeg when his aunt has a toothache

works.name: The Interlopers
works.description: Two men, Georg Znaeym and Ulrich von Gradwitz, who have a generations-long land feud, get trapped under a fallen tree in the Carpathians. They reconcile and share a wine flask, imagining riding into the market square together. The story ends with the chilling one-word sentence 'Wolves.' implying they will be eaten.

clues.clue: Ends with the one-word sentence 'Wolves.'
examples: ends with the one-word sentence 'Wolves.'

clues.clue: Znaeym and von Gradwitz feud; trapped under a fallen tree; reconcile
examples: two men trapped under a tree reconcile the feud between the Znaeym and von Gradwitz families

clues.clue: They share a wine flask and imagine riding into the market square together
examples: share a wine flask and imagine what it would be like if they 'rode into the market square together'

works.name: Tobermory
works.description: A story in which Cornelius Appin teaches the title cat to speak human language. The cat turns out to be a malicious gossip, revealing his owners' secrets at Mrs. Blemley's party. The cat conveniently dies, preventing further embarrassment. One character 'deserved all he got' for trying to teach German irregular verbs to elephants.

clues.clue: Cornelius Appin teaches the title cat to speak; cat reveals owners' secrets
examples: Cornelius Appin teaches the title cat to talk, and he turns out to be a malicious gossip

clues.clue: One character 'deserved all he got' for teaching German irregular verbs to elephants
examples: 'deserved all he got' for trying to teach German irregular verbs to elephants

works.name: The Lumber Room
works.description: Nicholas is punished for putting a frog in his breakfast and later discovers a large tapestry depicting a stag hunt. He refuses to rescue his aunt from a rainwater tank, pretending she might be the Devil in disguise. Appears in the collection Beasts and Superbeasts.

clues.clue: Nicholas puts a frog in his breakfast; discovers tapestry of a stag hunt
examples: Nicholas is punished for putting a frog in his breakfast and later discovers a tapestry depicting a stag hunt

clues.clue: Boy refuses to rescue aunt from rainwater tank, pretending she is the Devil
examples: refuses to rescue his aunt from a rainwater tank, claiming she might be the Devil

works.name: The Schartz-Metterklume Method
works.description: A woman pretending to be a governess (Lady Carlotta, dismissed from teaching by Mrs. Quabarl) teaches children history by making them reenact the Rape of the Sabine Women. This woman pretends to keep a leopard cub in her luggage.

clues.clue: Woman pretending to be a governess makes children reenact the Rape of the Sabine Women
examples: a woman pretending to be a governess teaches children history by making them reenact the Rape of the Sabine Women

clues.clue: Pretends to keep a leopard cub in her luggage; dismissed by Mrs. Quabarl
examples: pretends to keep a leopard cub in her luggage

works.name: The Unrest-Cure
works.description: The recurring character Clovis Sangrail poses as Stanislaus, the secretary of a Bishop, who is supposedly plotting to massacre Jews, in order to administer an 'unrest-cure' to the complacent, routine-addicted J. P. Huddle.

clues.clue: Clovis pretends to plot a massacre of Jews to cure J. P. Huddle's addiction to routine
examples: pretending to plot a massacre of all the Jews living in the neighborhood

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Pen name comes from the Rubaiyat's word for 'cupbearer.' Real name Hector Hugh Munro. Edwardian British author. Early career included reworking Alice in Wonderland into political satires for the Westminster Gazette (with Francis Carruthers Gould). Parodied Shaw's Man and Superman with his collection Beasts and Super-Beasts. Recurring characters include Clovis Sangrail (from The Chronicles of Clovis) and Reginald. Sandie Byrne coined the 'feral ephebe' trope for his early protagonists. Other stories include 'Esme' (a hyena eats a child), 'Laura' (reincarnation as an otter), 'The Storyteller' (bachelor tells children about a 'horribly good' girl eaten by a wolf), 'Gabriel-Ernest' (werewolf), 'The Wolves of Cernogratz,' 'The East Wing,' 'The Toys of Peace,' 'The She-Wolf,' and 'The Occasional Garden.'

clues.clue: Pen name from the Rubaiyat's word for 'cupbearer'
examples: took his pen name from the Rubaiyat's word for 'cupbearer'

comprehensive_summary:
Saki (1870-1916), the pen name of Hector Hugh Munro, was an Edwardian British short story writer renowned for dark humor, sardonic wit, and twist endings. His pen name derives from the Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam's word for 'cupbearer.'

'The Open Window' is his most frequently clued story. In it, the young Vera lies to the nervous visitor Framton Nuttel about the deaths of three hunters and a dog who supposedly disappeared into a bog. When they return through the open window, Nuttel flees in terror, and the story concludes with the iconic line 'Romance at short notice was her specialty.' Vera then smoothly explains Nuttel's departure as due to a 'horror of dogs.'

'Sredni Vashtar' features the boy Conradin who forms a cult around a polecat-ferret he names Sredni Vashtar. He steals powdered nutmeg as a reward for causing his guardian's toothache. The ferret kills his cruel guardian Mrs. De Ropp, and the story ends with Conradin calmly making toast while hearing the maid's screams.

'The Interlopers' depicts the feud between Georg Znaeym and Ulrich von Gradwitz, who get trapped under a fallen tree in the Carpathians. They reconcile and share a wine flask, only for the story to end with the devastating one-word sentence 'Wolves.' 'Tobermory' features Cornelius Appin teaching a cat to speak, who then reveals everyone's secrets at Mrs. Blemley's party.

'The Lumber Room' follows Nicholas, punished for putting a frog in his breakfast, who discovers a tapestry depicting a stag hunt and refuses to rescue his aunt from a rainwater tank, pretending she is the Devil. 'The Schartz-Metterklume Method' features a woman pretending to be a governess who makes children reenact the Rape of the Sabine Women. 'The Unrest-Cure' sees the recurring character Clovis Sangrail pretend to plot a massacre to cure J. P. Huddle's complacency.

Saki's recurring characters include the witty Clovis Sangrail (from The Chronicles of Clovis) and the earlier Reginald. His collection Beasts and Super-Beasts parodied Shaw's Man and Superman. His early career included reworking Alice in Wonderland into political satires for the Westminster Gazette. Other notable stories include 'Esme' (a hyena eats a child), 'Laura' (reincarnation as an otter), 'The Storyteller' (a bachelor tells children about a 'horribly good' girl eaten by a wolf), 'Gabriel-Ernest' (a werewolf story), 'The Wolves of Cernogratz,' 'The East Wing,' 'The Toys of Peace,' and 'The Occasional Garden.'
